name: Vitamin K prophylaxis for newborns
comparator: No prophylaxis, placebo, or oral regimen
summary: Routine vitamin K at birth is associated with a large reduction in vitamin K deficiency bleeding, including the severe late form, with no consistent evidence of harm.
description: Vitamin K deficiency bleeding (VKDB), historically called hemorrhagic disease of the newborn, results from the physiologically low vitamin K stores of neonates. It is classified as early (first 24 hours), classic (days 1–7), and late (weeks 2–12). Late VKDB carries a high risk of intracranial hemorrhage and death. A single dose of vitamin K at birth, particularly by the intramuscular route, raises vitamin K–dependent clotting factor activity and is used worldwide as prophylaxis.
methodologyNotes: The numeric event counts in this topic are ILLUSTRATIVE placeholders used to demonstrate the analysis pipeline and have not been verified against the source publications. Study identities (authors, years, citations) are real, but the 2×2 counts must be replaced with verified extractions before any clinical interpretation. Classic and late VKDB are rare events, so several historical data points derive from surveillance registries rather than randomized trials; these are entered as precomputed effects and some are excluded from pooling.

outcomes.label: Any VKDB / clinical bleeding
outcomes.description: Any clinically significant vitamin K deficiency bleeding event.

outcomes.label: Late VKDB
outcomes.description: VKDB occurring after the first week of life, often intracranial.

outcomes.label: Biochemical deficiency
outcomes.description: Abnormal coagulation indices (e.g. elevated PIVKA-II, prolonged PT).
